summary:
Joseph Lister's development of antiseptic surgical techniques in 1867 was one of the most immediately life-saving medical innovations in history. Before Lister's work, hospital surgical wards were death traps: post-operative infection rates were catastrophically high, and many surgeons accepted 'ward fever' and 'hospital gangrene' as inevitable aspects of surgery. Lister's application of [[event:2579|germ theory]] to surgical practice transformed surgery from a frequently fatal intervention into a relatively safe one.

Lister was working as a surgeon in Glasgow when he read Louis Pasteur's work on microorganisms as the cause of putrefaction and fermentation. He reasoned that the wound infections killing his patients were caused by microorganisms entering surgical wounds, and that killing these organisms with a chemical agent would prevent infection. He chose carbolic acid (phenol) as his antiseptic agent and began applying it to surgical instruments, wound dressings, the surgical field, and his own hands.

The results were dramatic. Lister published his findings in The Lancet in 1867, reporting that his mortality rate for compound fractures (where bone breaks through the skin) had fallen from approximately 46 percent to 15 percent using antiseptic technique. Not all surgeons immediately accepted his methods — some doubted germ theory, others found carbolic acid caustic and unpleasant to work with — but the evidence was compelling and adoption spread progressively through the 1870s.

Lister's work opened the door to surgery that had previously been considered too risky. Operations on the abdomen, chest, and brain became possible when infection could be controlled. His methods were refined into 'aseptic' surgery (preventing microorganisms from entering wounds in the first place rather than killing them) by the 1880s and 1890s. Lister is widely regarded as the father of modern surgery and was elevated to the peerage as Baron Lister — the first surgeon so honored in Britain.
